summary: Yegge recounts the chaotic behind-the-scenes story of how he came to give an OSCON keynote talk about marketing and branding. The piece weaves together his attendance at Foo Camp, where he witnessed OSI's futile attempt to protect the 'Open Source' trademark, with his last-minute preparation for a keynote he didn't expect anyone to attend. He discovers too late that he's the closing keynote speaker for 3,000 people, gets shepherded through a surreal backstage experience by a judgmental handler, and faces technical difficulties right as his talk begins. The post ends on a cliffhanger, promising the actual talk content in a follow-up.
key_points: The OSI's attempt to protect 'Open Source' through federation and angry letters is doomed because the term was never trademarked and has no clear definition, unlike Stallman's 'Free Software'
Marketing isn't about banner ads—it's about what your friends are saying, as illustrated by the mountain bike seat that failed because pros mocked it
Yegge chose to talk about marketing despite knowing almost nothing about it, reasoning he'd read one marketing book which was one more than most programmers
The behind-the-scenes reality of keynote speaking involves sneaky technicians, lost dongles, and crawling to the front row to avoid blocking the projector
money_quotes: Marketing. It's not the banner ads. It's what your friends are saying.
If it's not trademarked, and you can't protect it legally, and it's just two vanilla words concatenated, then you're screwed.
Me? I was all for changing the name. What the hell does Open Source mean, anyway?
I decided, then and there, with me not knowing Jack Squat about marketing, to do my OSCON presentation on marketing.
He walks past me, stops, looks me up and down with the exact same expression the restaurant waiters in Paris used on me.
At Foo Camp when someone's giving a talk that sucks, it's kind of hard to escape politely.
key_insight: The open source movement's branding problem stems from failing to clearly define and legally protect what 'Open Source' actually means, leaving the term vulnerable to corporate co-optation.
